specification.topic-statement: The database schema migration system applies SQL scripts in version order to bring the schema to the current version.

scope.in-scope: Version detection, migration file discovery and parsing, ordered execution of pending migration SQL scripts, and error reporting.
scope.boundary: This topic submits SQL strings to the database connection and receives status codes and error messages in return. The database enforces transactional semantics within each migration file; this topic does not control transaction boundaries.
This topic reads the contents of migration files identified by scanning a directory. File access errors are translated into domain-specific error messages and returned to the caller.
This topic receives an established PostgreSQL connection. Opening, configuring, or closing the connection is outside this scope.

field.name: migration number
field.note: extracted from filename prefix; 3 or 4 decimal digits

field.name: outcome
field.note: OK on full success; ERR with IO code on file system failure; ERR with DB_MIGRATE code on SQL execution failure

behavior.detecting-current-version:
Before examining any migration files, the system queries the schema metadata table for the current schema version. The query retrieves at most one row.
If the schema metadata table does not yet exist, the query fails with a database error. This condition is treated as version zero — the same as a fresh, empty database. No error is reported.
If the table exists and contains at least one row, the version value from the first returned row is parsed as a decimal integer. If the value parses cleanly (no trailing non-digit characters, non-negative, within integer range), it is used as the current version.
If the stored version value fails to parse as a valid non-negative integer, the current version is treated as zero. No error is reported.
A corrupted or out-of-range version value silently causes all migrations to be re-applied from the beginning, which may fail if the schema already partially exists.
behavior.discovering-migration-files:
The migrations directory is opened for reading. If the directory cannot be opened, an IO error is returned immediately with no migrations applied.
Each directory entry is examined. An entry is accepted as a migration file only when its name satisfies all of the following conditions:
- The name is at least 9 characters long. - The name ends with the exact suffix ".sql". - The name begins with exactly 3 or 4 consecutive decimal digit characters. - The character immediately following the digit prefix is a hyphen "-". - The digit prefix parses as a non-negative integer within the range of a signed integer.
The standard directory pseudo-entries "." and ".." are skipped without parsing.
Any entry that fails any of these conditions is silently skipped. No warning or error is produced. A file named "002_wrong-separator.sql" (underscore instead of hyphen) or "002-missing-extension.txt" is discarded without notice.
All accepted entries are sorted in ascending order by their numeric migration number before any SQL is executed.
behavior.applying-pending-migrations:
Each accepted migration entry is examined in sorted order. A migration is applied only when its number is strictly greater than the current version. Migrations whose number is less than or equal to the current version are skipped without reading their files.
The SQL file is read into memory in full. If the file cannot be opened, its size cannot be determined, or its contents cannot be read, an IO error is returned immediately. The specific error message varies by failure mode:
- Cannot open: "Cannot open migration file" - Cannot seek within the file: "Cannot seek migration file" - Cannot determine file size: "Cannot get migration file size" - File exceeds the maximum readable size: "Migration file too large" - Any other read failure: "Failed to read migration file"
The full file contents are submitted to the database as a single statement string. The database executes them in one call.
Each migration file is responsible for its own transaction boundaries. The migration system does not wrap the file contents in BEGIN or COMMIT. If a migration file omits transaction control, partial changes may be committed if the file fails partway through execution.
After execution, the status of the database response is inspected. The execution is considered successful only when the status indicates either a successful command or a result set. Any other status causes an immediate DB_MIGRATE error return carrying the database's own error message and the migration number that failed. Subsequent migrations are not attempted.
When a migration fails, all previously applied migrations in the same run remain committed. There is no rollback of successfully completed migrations. The database is left in a partially-migrated state requiring manual recovery.
The migration system itself does not update the schema metadata table after each migration. Version advancement is expected to be part of the SQL in the migration file. If a migration file omits the version update, the current version remains unchanged after that migration executes, and subsequent runs re-apply the same migration.
Incorrect or missing version update statements within migration files can cause migrations to be executed repeatedly on subsequent runs.
behavior.successful-completion: When all pending migrations have been applied without error, the system returns success. All temporary working memory is released.
